title: What Comes After the Rage Has Spoken
dek: Your rage is not a wild animal to be caged or a moral failing to be ashamed of. It is a raw, pulsing message from your body’s deep architecture, demanding that you listen. But what happens when the fire dies down, when the voice of rage has been heard? This exploration guides you through the somatic landscape of what unfolds after fury’s initial eruption, weaving herbal wisdom, embodied practices, and the teachings of sorrow and healing into a map for your next steps.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Aftermath of Rage: A Body in Transition


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:43.170Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

You feel it - the heat slowly receding from your face, the clenching of your masseter muscles loosening, the sharpness in your diaphragm softening as the breath begins to return to its natural rhythm. The rage that once roared through your body now simmers like embers under cold ash. This is not a moment to rush past or smooth over with a quick apology or a forced smile. It is a crucial, tender space where your body is rewriting the story of what just passed. The trapezius, that steel cable running from your neck to your shoulder, might remain taut, a reminder that your nervous system is still on high alert. The levator scapulae, which lifted your scapula in that instinctive shrug of defense, might insist on holding tension just a little longer. You might notice a metallic taste lingering in your mouth, a trace of the adrenaline fizzing through your veins.

This post-rage moment is a somatic crossroads. It is when your body decides how it will digest the fire, how it will carry the memory, and how it might begin to heal. Rage speaks in muscle fibers and fascia, in the quickened pulse behind your eyes and the subtle tremor in your fingers. It is a messenger, not a villain.

## Listening to the Body’s Afterword: The Jaw Remembers

The jaw remembers. This is not a poetic metaphor but a biological reality grounded in fascia and neurology. The masseter and temporalis muscles, which have clenched in defense or fury, might continue to contract long after the words have fallen silent. This residual tension is a message in itself, a holding pattern that says, "I am still here, still vigilant." Robert Augustus Masters, a somatic therapist who has written extensively on trauma and embodiment, teaches that the body’s aftershocks are not to be rushed or ignored. He insists that the undoing of trauma and rage is a process of slow unwinding, like the gradual release of a twisted rope. Masters encourages us to locate these lingering tensions with curiosity, not judgment, to listen to what the body is saying beneath the noise of thought.

When you place your fingers gently along your jawline, you may notice a faint pulsing or a stubborn hardness where the body is still holding its story. This is the body’s way of keeping you safe, even if it no longer serves your well-being. The fascia, that dense connective tissue network, remembers every contraction, every clench, every suppressed scream. It is not pathology; it is biology. The body archives rage as a form of self-protection, and it requires patient, somatic care to unwind.

## The Herbal Ally: Calming the Nervous System and Soothing the Fascia

Herbal medicine offers a profound way to support the body in this delicate post-rage phase. The nervous system, flooded with catecholamines and cortisol, needs gentle yet effective allies to return to its baseline. Herbs like [Pure Encapsulations Daily Calm](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0DLCX6T4K?tag=spankyspinola-20) and [Gaia Herbs Calm A.S.A.P.](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07H2QFMNR?tag=spankyspinola-20) are revered in Traditional Chinese Medicine and Western herbalism alike for their nervine properties - they calm the overactive nervous system without sedating the mind. Passionflower, by modulating GABA receptors, helps ease the diaphragm's spasms, allowing the breath to find its natural depth again.

Beyond the nervous system, fascia and muscle require circulation and lubrication to release chronic holding patterns. Herbal infusions rich in magnesium, like nettle tea, can be a subtle but powerful ally. Nettle’s earthy aroma and mineral content feed the connective tissue, encouraging pliability and softness. Topical applications of arnica or St. John's Wort oil can be applied with mindful touch to the jaw, neck, and shoulders to reduce inflammation and promote healing at the site of muscular tension.

One ritual I often recommend is a warm herbal bath steeped with lavender, chamomile, and a pinch of crushed rosemary. This combination not only relaxes muscles but also signals to the parasympathetic nervous system that it is safe to descend into rest. The scent, the warmth, and the tactile sensation of water on skin create a multisensory container for the body to release what it no longer needs.

## The Teachings of Thich Nhat Hanh: Mindful Acknowledgment of Rage’s Echoes

Thich Nhat Hanh, the Vietnamese Zen master and mindfulness teacher, offers a compassionate framework for sitting with the aftermath of rage. He teaches that every emotion, even the most turbulent fury, deserves to be met with mindful presence rather than repression or reaction. His practice of "mindful breathing" is not about suppressing anger but about making space for it to reveal its message.

After the rage has spoken, you can return to the breath as an anchor in the storm’s wake. Feel the rise and fall of your diaphragm, notice the slight expansion of the intercostal muscles, the subtle stretch of the fascia lining your ribcage. Thich Nhat Hanh’s instructions emphasize the importance of "hugging" the wounded part of yourself with kindness, acknowledging that the fire was a form of protection, a boundary set fiercely when words or softer expressions failed.

In this light, the tension in your trapezius or the tightness in your jaw becomes a signal, not a failure. It is the body’s way of saying, "I was in danger," or "I was unheard." Through gentle mindfulness, you cultivate an inner container spacious enough to hold these sensations without judgment, allowing the nervous system to recalibrate naturally.

## Resmaa Menakem and the Racialized Body of Rage

Resmaa Menakem’s work, especially in his book *My Grandmother’s Hands*, offers a powerful lens on how rage and its aftermath are embodied in racialized bodies. Menakem emphasizes that the trauma of white supremacy lives in the muscles and fascia, in the ancestral memory of bodies that have been conditioned to carry rage as a survival mechanism. His approach insists that healing requires somatic practices that awaken the body’s inherent capacity to regulate and soothe.

Menakem invites you to notice where rage settles in your own body - not just as a conceptual feeling but as a physical reality. For some, it might be the tension in the psoas, the deep core muscle that tightens when you feel threatened. For others, it might be the shallow, rapid breathing that keeps the diaphragm in a state of contraction. This is not about willpower or moral correction; it is about biology and the nervous system’s deep wisdom.

In the aftermath of rage, Menakem encourages practices that re-establish connection to the body’s ground - literal grounding exercises that engage the feet and legs, or slow, intentional stretches that invite the fascia to release. He teaches that the body can learn to hold rage without being overwhelmed by it, that you can reclaim your nervous system one breath, one movement, and one touch at a time.

## An Embodied Practice: Listening to Your Rage’s Afterglow

Find a quiet space where you can sit or lie down comfortably. Place one hand gently on your jawline and the other on your diaphragm, just below the ribs. Close your eyes if that feels safe. Begin by noticing the residual sensations - the tightness in your masseter, the subtle rise and fall of your breath, the heat or coolness of your skin.

Without trying to change anything, breathe slowly and deeply into your diaphragm. As you inhale, imagine sending your breath to the tight muscles around your jaw and neck. As you exhale, visualize those muscles softening, the fascia releasing its grip. If a memory or image arises, allow it to be there without pushing it away.

Stay with this cycle for five to ten minutes. When you finish, gently open your eyes and take a moment to notice any shifts in your body or mood. This practice is not about erasing rage but about honoring its presence and inviting your body to move toward ease.

## The Role of Time and Patience: Healing is Not a Deadline

Rage is a wildfire, but healing is the slow growth of forest after the burn. It is essential to honor the timing of your body’s process. The fascia that remembers tension does not unwind overnight, and the nervous system that has been flooded with stress chemicals requires consistent care to return to balance. This is not a failure or a sign of weakness; this is what it means to be human.

In her teachings, Pema Chödrön reminds us that "when we are willing to sit with discomfort, with the unknown, with the uncomfortableness of our emotions, we open the door to transformation." The discomfort you feel after rage has spoken is a doorway, not a wall. Your body is inviting you to step inside, to witness, and to learn.

You might find that certain herbal allies become your companions over weeks or months. A tincture like [Host Defense Lion's Mane Powder](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07TJ63MH3?tag=spankyspinola-20) can be taken at night to deepen restorative sleep, while a daily tea of lemon balm can gently ease daytime anxiety. Using these herbs with respect and intention - recognizing their power, their specific actions on the nervous system and musculature - supports your body’s natural wisdom.

## Integrating the Message: What Rage Reveals Beyond the Heat

When the fire of rage cools, what remains is a message from your boundary lines, your unmet needs, your deeply held truths. Rage is never random; it is a form of communication from your body’s core. The post-rage phase is a time for integration - where you reflect on what this fierce messenger has shown you.

Gabor Maté’s work on trauma and emotional regulation offers insight here. He teaches that anger often masks pain and grief that have long been denied expression. To truly listen to what comes after rage is to begin a dialogue with those hidden parts of the self. It is to recognize that your nervous system has been trying to protect you from further injury, even if the method was intense.

This integration is not about intellectualizing or rationalizing your fury but about connecting somatically with its roots. You might journal, meditate, or simply sit in quiet awareness, but always return to the body. Notice how your psoas or trapezius feels. Notice where your breath moves freely and where it hesitates. This is the landscape where your healing unfolds.



## The Quiet Aftershock: Navigating the Nervous System’s Lingering Echoes

The rage has passed, yet your body remains a landscape marked by its recent storm. It’s 2:17 a.m., the house is silent except for the distant hum of a refrigerator, and you lie awake, aware of a tautness threading through your levator scapulae and the subtle tremor that won’t settle in your fingers. This is the nervous system’s echo - an aftershock reverberating through the autonomic pathways long after the conscious mind has turned away from the fire. It’s not unusual for the sympathetic nervous system to hold its “fight or flight” state longer than the original trigger, especially when you’ve been trained to deem your fury unacceptable or unsafe to express.

This lingering sympathetic arousal is felt in the microdetails of your body: the tiny muscle spasms beneath the skin of your forearms, the persistent shallow breath that barely moves the diaphragm, the cold sweat pooling at the small of your back under the elastic band of your pajama pants. Your vagus nerve - the wandering nerve that mediates parasympathetic calming - is sluggish, its tone diminished by the flood of stress hormones coursing through your bloodstream. This is not a failure of will or character. This is biology, a nervous system designed to protect but now stuck in a state of hypervigilance.

Resmaa Menakem’s insistence on somatic grounding becomes vital here. You may find yourself drawn to standing barefoot on the cold kitchen tiles, feeling the solid, unyielding earth beneath your feet. The pressure of gravity on your soles communicates safety in a language your brain cannot fabricate - “You are here. You are supported.” This simple act begins to engage the parasympathetic nervous system, coaxing your levator scapulae and trapezius to release their iron grip. The legs, often neglected in emotional processing, can be the gateway to down-regulating the nervous system’s relentless watchfulness.

In these early morning hours, a cup of chamomile tea can serve as a gentle somatic anchor. Chamomile’s apigenin content binds to benzodiazepine receptors in the brain, promoting a sense of calm without dulling awareness. Sip it slowly, noting the warmth spreading through your hands, the faint herbal sweetness lingering on your tongue, the steam that carries floral notes into your nasal passages. This ritual, small and deliberate, signals to your entire system that it is safe to descend from the heights of anxiety and rage.

## The Breath’s Lingering Imprint: Reclaiming the Diaphragm’s Voice

The diaphragm, that dome-shaped muscle beneath your lungs, is the epicenter of rage’s somatic imprint. When fury flares, the diaphragm often spasms or contracts irregularly, caught between the primal urges to fight, flee, or freeze. After the initial storm, this muscle can remain locked in a shallow, uneven rhythm, constraining the flow of life-giving air and perpetuating a sense of internal constriction and unrest.

Notice the precise sensation of your breath in the aftermath: is it a hesitant fluttering at the base of the lungs? Do you feel the intercostal muscles struggle to expand the ribcage fully? The fascia surrounding the diaphragm may be thickened or restricted, as if it carries the weight of all the unspoken rage you’ve been taught to suppress. This is the body’s way of protecting itself from further harm, but it also limits your capacity to fully inhabit your body and express your truth.

Karla McLaren’s work with emotional awareness invites you to become an embodied witness to these breath patterns without trying to fix or force them. Instead, imagine your breath as a soft wave washing over the diaphragm’s landscape, coaxing it to remember its natural rhythm. Place your hands gently on your lower ribs, feeling the subtle expansion and contraction as you inhale and exhale. This tactile feedback reconnects your nervous system with the body’s inherent capacity for self-regulation.

Herbs like skullcap and holy basil can support this process. Skullcap’s mild muscle-relaxing properties ease the diaphragm’s spasms, while holy basil’s adaptogenic nature helps modulate the stress response, encouraging a smoother breath cycle. A tincture taken in the early evening, combined with a focused breathing practice, can gradually retrain the diaphragm to reclaim its full, expressive range.

You might also explore gentle movement practices - slow, intentional yoga poses that open the chest and lengthen the intercostal muscles. The sphinx pose, for instance, softly stretches the fascia over the diaphragm while inviting mindful breathing. The sensation of your diaphragm expanding against gravity, the subtle engagement of your psoas, and the awakening of your core’s connective tissue network all signal that your body is ready to move beyond the tightness of rage.

## The Sacredness of Tender Touch: Herbs and Rituals for Fascial Release

When the rage has spoken, the fascia - the continuous web of connective tissue that encases every muscle, bone, and organ - often remains bound in the shape of your fury. This tissue is highly innervated and responsive to emotional states; it literally thickens and tightens in response to chronic stress or unexpressed anger. The jaw, the neck, and the shoulders are common repositories for this fascial holding.

To address this, we turn to the medicine of touch, guided by herbal allies that penetrate deeply into the tissues. A warming oil infused with arnica and St. John’s Wort can be massaged into these areas with slow, deliberate strokes. The warmth from the oil, the scent of the herbs - bitter, bright, and slightly piney - are invitations for the fascia to soften. Arnica’s anti-inflammatory properties reduce localized swelling, while St. John’s Wort’s neuroregenerative effects support the nervous system’s healing.

You might create a ritual around this practice: lighting a beeswax candle whose faint honey scent mingles with the herbal aroma, playing a quiet recording of a river’s flow or a gentle rain. As you apply the oil to your jaw and neck, notice the subtle release in the masseter muscle, the way the skin warms under your touch, and the slow unwinding of tension that has been held for far too long.

In Traditional Chinese Medicine, the liver channel runs through the sides of the body, governing the smooth flow of qi and emotions. When rage is stuck, so too is the qi. Gentle gua sha or moxibustion along the gallbladder meridian - running from the temple down the side of the neck and shoulder - can encourage circulation and energetic balance. These modalities honor the body’s wisdom, treating the fascia not as passive tissue but as an active participant in emotional processing.

Herbal infusions of nettle and oatstraw drunk throughout the day provide mineral support to the connective tissue, nourishing the fascia’s collagen matrix. The minerals magnesium and calcium play essential roles in muscle relaxation and contraction; their replenishment is not just biochemical but felt in the subtle shifts of your body’s ease.

This is not about quick fixes or superficial comfort. This is about entering into a conversation with your body’s deep biology, honoring the dense, unspoken language of fascia, and offering it the medicine of touch, herbs, and ritual. Rage is a messenger that arrives in muscle and tendon, and it departs on the breath and the gentle hand of care.

---

Your rage has spoken, and now the body whispers its afterword in muscle fibers and fascia, in breath patterns and nervous system rhythms. This is a biological narrative, not a moral one. The body knows what to do if you listen - not with your mind, but with your hands on your jaw, your feet on cool earth, your breath moving through the diaphragm’s dome. The medicine is slow, patient, and reverent. The fire may burn hot and wild, but the healing unfolds in the quiet spaces that follow.

## FAQ

**Q: Is it normal to still feel physical tension days after an episode of rage?**  
**A:** Absolutely. The body’s muscles and fascia often hold onto tension long after the emotional intensity has passed. This is a survival mechanism, not a sign of weakness. Gentle somatic practices and herbal supports can assist in the gradual unwinding of this tension.

**Q: Can herbal remedies replace therapy or other forms of emotional support?**  
**A:** Herbs are powerful allies but are best used in conjunction with somatic therapies, counseling, or other forms of emotional work. They support the body’s biology but do not replace the need for addressing the underlying emotional landscape.

**Q: How can I tell if my rage is healthy expression or a sign of deeper trauma?**  
**A:** Healthy rage serves clear boundaries and communicates needs without overwhelming your nervous system. If your rage leaves you feeling depleted, frozen, or disconnected, it may be linked to deeper trauma. Working with somatic therapists or trauma-informed practitioners can help clarify this.

**Q: What if I feel shame or guilt after my rage?**  
**A:** Shame and guilt are common but often reflect societal conditioning rather than your true self. The body remembers and can help you untangle these feelings through compassionate, embodied practices. Remember, your rage is a messenger, not a moral failure.

**Q: How do I begin to listen to my body if I’ve been taught to suppress anger?**  
**A:** Start with small, gentle practices like mindful breathing or placing your hand on your jaw or diaphragm to notice sensations. Over time, as you become more familiar with your body’s signals, you can explore deeper somatic work, ideally with the guidance of a skilled practitioner.

**Q: Are there risks to sitting with rage in the body?**  
**A:** Like any intense emotional work, sitting with rage should be approached with care and, when possible, support. If you experience overwhelming symptoms or trauma responses, seek guidance from a trauma-informed therapist or somatic guide.

Your rage has spoken. Now, your body invites you to listen - to the tremors, the tightness, the breath, and the wisdom beneath it all. This is not a journey of quick fixes, but a slow, sacred unfolding of your body’s knowing.

---
